explicit_text: I was born in Carthage, Texas, not in a hospital but in a doctor's clinic run by Dr. Hooker. My family lived six miles outside of Carthage at a natural gas company compressor station before moving back into town after my father returned from World War II. I attended school in Carthage for twelve years, riding the school bus every day. Carthage was my hometown and the center of much of my early life and family history.
implicit_text: I was born in a small East Texas town, not in a hospital but in a doctor's clinic run by Dr. Hooker. My family lived six miles outside of this town at a natural gas company compressor station before moving back into town after my father returned from World War II. I attended school there for twelve years, riding the school bus every day. This town was my hometown and the center of much of my early life and family history.

explicit_text: My mother, Tula Hightower, was born in St. Augustine, Texas, and moved to Carthage as a child. She was a sharecropper's daughter during the Great Depression and later became deeply involved in the church and community. Despite not being raised in the church, she became a devoted Christian and active in the United Methodist Women. She also wrote a weekly devotional column for the Panola Watchmen for twenty-five years and was a fitness enthusiast well into her sixties and seventies.
implicit_text: My mother was born in a small farming community in East Texas and moved to the town where I grew up as a child. She was a sharecropper's daughter during the Great Depression and later became deeply involved in the church and community. Despite not being raised in the church, she became a devoted Christian and active in the Methodist women's organization. She also wrote a weekly devotional column for the local weekly newspaper for twenty-five years and was a fitness enthusiast well into her sixties and seventies.

explicit_text: My mother was born in St. Augustine, Texas, which is about sixty miles south of Carthage. Her family moved from there to Carthage when she was a child, so although I was born in Carthage, my mother's roots trace back to St. Augustine. It was a farming community where her family worked as sharecroppers during the Great Depression.
implicit_text: My mother was born in a small East Texas farming community about sixty miles south of the town where I was born. Her family moved from there to that town when she was a child, so although I was born in the town, my mother's roots trace back to that farming community. It was a place where her family worked as sharecroppers during the Great Depression.

explicit_text: My mother's family were sharecroppers during the Great Depression, working very hard but only getting half of what they produced. My father also grew up during that time and had to leave school to help support his family. The hardships of the Great Depression shaped both my parents' values and their determination to see their children go to college.
implicit_text: My mother's family were sharecroppers during the economic hardships of the 1930s, working very hard but only getting half of what they produced. My father also grew up during that time and had to leave school to help support his family. The hardships of that era shaped both my parents' values and their determination to see their children go to college.

explicit_text: I was just a baby when the Japanese bombed Pearl Harbor in December 1941, which marked the beginning of the United States' involvement in World War II. My father was drafted two years later in 1943 and sent off to fight. That event changed our family life dramatically, as we had to move out of our company house and live with my grandparents during the war.
implicit_text: I was just a baby when the Japanese attacked the naval base in Hawaii in December 1941, which marked the beginning of the United States' involvement in World War II. My father was drafted two years later in 1943 and sent off to fight. That event changed our family life dramatically, as we had to move out of our company house and live with my grandparents during the war.
cues: start of World War II for US

explicit_text: World War II had a big impact on my family. My father was drafted in 1943 and served in the 86th Infantry Division in Germany, part of Patton's Third Army during the Battle of Bastogne. After the war in Europe ended, his unit was sent to the Philippines to prepare for the invasion of Japan. When he returned, our family moved back to the company house near Carthage, and life slowly returned to normal.
implicit_text: The global conflict of the early 1940s had a big impact on my family. My father was drafted in 1943 and served in an infantry division in Germany, part of General Patton's Third Army during the Battle of Bastogne. After the war in Europe ended, his unit was sent to the Pacific to prepare for the invasion of Japan. When he returned, our family moved back to the company house near the town where I grew up, and life slowly returned to normal.

explicit_text: My father fought in Germany during World War II with the 86th Infantry Division. He was part of Patton’s Third Army that rushed to Bastogne in the bitter winter of 1944. He saw a lot of action there in the final year of the war in Europe before being sent to the Philippines afterward.

explicit_text: My mother became very involved in the church after the war, especially with the United Methodist Women, which was known then as the Women's Society of Christian Service. She attended meetings regularly and was active in church life, including Sunday services and prayer meetings. Her faith and church involvement deeply influenced our family.

explicit_text: My mother dreamed that all her children would attend Southern Methodist University, or SMU, because it was big and Methodist and she believed that made it good. I, my sister Jacqueline, and my brother Warren all graduated from SMU, and now both of my sons are SMU graduates as well. SMU has been a central part of our family's educational journey.

That university has been a central part of our family's educational journey.

explicit_text: My sister Jacqueline married a Texas A&M engineer named Manual Rodriguez, and their two sons are also Aggie graduates. So Texas A&M became part of our extended family's educational story, complementing the SMU tradition in our immediate family.

explicit_text: My brother Warren was just the right age to be drafted into the Vietnam War. He served with distinction and came back to become a Dallas police officer before eventually returning to school at SMU for advanced banking studies. His service in Vietnam was a significant chapter in his life and ours.

explicit_text: After returning from Vietnam, my brother Warren worked as a Dallas police officer for eight years. He found it difficult to make a living as a policeman, so he went back to school at SMU and later became a bank president in our hometown. His time with the Dallas Police Department was an important part of his career path.

explicit_text: My mother wrote a weekly devotional column called 'First Things First' for the Panola Watchman, a small weekly paper in Carthage. She started after someone suggested publishing her devotional for Church Women United, and it became very popular, running for twenty-five years. That column was a significant way she contributed to our community.

explicit_text: I once read in the National Education Association Journal that a child who lives with praise will develop a sense of destiny. This idea resonated with me because my mother always told me and my siblings that we were special and had a purpose. That encouragement helped shape my belief that we were sent to this time and place to do something significant.

explicit_text: My father’s full name was Marvin Mouzon Biggs Sr., named after two Methodist bishops, Enoch Marvin and Edwin Dubose Mouzon. He was the only son in his family and worked for a natural gas company for forty-three years, eventually becoming superintendent of East Texas operations. He never finished high school but was a respected community member who served on the hospital board and deeply valued education for his children.

explicit_text: Enoch Marvin was one of the two Methodist bishops after whom my father was named. My grandparents had heard him preach and honored him by giving his name to my father. This connection to Methodist heritage was important in our family identity.

After the war in Europe ended, his unit was sent to the Philippines to prepare for the invasion of Japan.
